subject: ap-chemistry
title: CaCO₃ + HCl: Kinetics, Stoichiometry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
Answer the following questions about an experiment in which $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$ is combined with $\text{HCl}(aq)$, represented by the following balanced equation.

$\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$

parts.prompt:
Write the balanced net ionic equation for the reaction.

A student performs an investigation to study factors that affect the rate of the reaction. In each trial the student combines 50.0 mL of $\text{HCl}(aq)$ at 21.2°C with 1.00 g of $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$ and measures the time required for the reaction to go to completion. The data are given in the following table.

| Trial | Concentration of HCl(aq) (M) | Particle Size of $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$ | Time of Reaction (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.00 | Fine powder | 67 |
| 2 | 1.00 | Small chunks | 112 |
| 3 | 1.00 | Large chunk | 342 |
| 4 | 3.00 | Fine powder | 22 |
| 5 | 3.00 | Small chunks | 227 |
| 6 | 3.00 | Large chunk | 114 |
parts.scoring_notes: Net ionic equation: eliminate spectator ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$). $\text{CaCO}_3$ is a solid (insoluble), so it stays molecular. $\text{CaCl}_2$ dissociates to $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ (spectators). Net ionic: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. (State symbols may be omitted.)

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Spectator ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Cl}^-$) are removed from the full ionic equation:
$\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$

(State symbols not required. $\text{CaCO}_3$ is an insoluble solid and is NOT split into ions.)

wrong_examples: Writing $\text{CO}_3^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ — this omits $\text{CaCO}_3$ as a solid; the solid is not dissociated.
Keeping $\text{Cl}^-$ and $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ in the equation — spectator ions do not appear in the net ionic equation.
common_traps: Net ionic equation: (1) write full ionic equation; (2) cancel spectator ions (those that appear unchanged on both sides). $\text{CaCO}_3(s)$ is insoluble → stays as solid formula in net ionic. $\text{HCl}$ is a strong acid → splits into $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$.

parts.prompt: The student correctly identifies that trial 5 is inconsistent with the other trials. Explain why the student's claim is correct using the data in the table.
parts.scoring_notes: Trial 5 (3.00 M HCl, small chunks, 227 s) is inconsistent. Higher HCl concentration should speed up the reaction (shorter time), but Trial 5 takes 227 s while Trial 2 (1.00 M HCl, small chunks) takes only 112 s — the higher concentration gave a longer time. All other paired comparisons show that increasing [HCl] decreases reaction time (Trials 1→4: 67 s vs. 22 s; Trials 3→6: 342 s vs. 114 s). Trial 5 contradicts this trend.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing Trial 5's inconsistency with the established trend: 1 point

required_elements: Trial 5 (3.00 M HCl, small chunks) is slower (227 s) than Trial 2 (1.00 M HCl, small chunks, 112 s), even though all other trials show higher [HCl] increases reaction rate.
alternatives.correct_example: Based on Trials 1 and 4 (fine powder, same particle size), increasing [HCl] from 1.00 M to 3.00 M decreases reaction time (67 s → 22 s). Similarly, Trials 3 and 6 show the same trend (342 s → 114 s). Trial 5 (3.00 M, small chunks) has a reaction time of 227 s, which is longer than Trial 2 (1.00 M, small chunks, 112 s) — the opposite of the expected trend. This makes Trial 5 inconsistent with the data pattern.

wrong_examples: Just saying the time is 'too long' without comparing to specific trials — must cite specific data values.
Comparing Trial 5 to a trial with different particle size — comparisons must control for particle size.
common_traps: To identify an inconsistency, compare trials that differ only in [HCl]. Trial 2 and Trial 5 use the same particle size (small chunks) but different [HCl]. Higher [HCl] should be faster, but Trial 5 is slower than Trial 2 — the inconsistency.

parts.prompt: Based on the reaction conditions and the collisions that occur between particles, explain the reason for the difference in the reaction times for trial 2 and trial 3.
parts.scoring_notes: Trials 2 and 3 both use 1.00 M HCl but differ in $\text{CaCO}_3$ particle size: small chunks (Trial 2, 112 s) vs. large chunk (Trial 3, 342 s). Smaller particles have greater total surface area exposed to HCl. More surface area means more $\text{CaCO}_3$ surface is available for contact with $\text{H}^+$ ions, leading to a higher frequency of effective collisions per unit time. More effective collisions per second → faster reaction → shorter time in Trial 2.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of surface area difference between trial 2 and trial 3: 1 point

required_elements: Small chunks (Trial 2) have greater surface area than large chunk (Trial 3).
Greater surface area exposes more $\text{CaCO}_3$ to the acid.
alternatives.correct_example: In Trial 2, the small chunks of $\text{CaCO}_3$ have a much larger total surface area exposed to the $\text{HCl}(aq)$ than the single large chunk in Trial 3. More surface area means more $\text{CaCO}_3$ is in contact with the acid at any given time.

wrong_examples: Saying the small chunks are more reactive — the chemical identity is identical; only physical surface area differs.
Citing concentration differences — Trials 2 and 3 use the same HCl concentration (1.00 M); only particle size differs.
common_traps: Same concentration and mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$ — only surface area differs. Small chunks = more surface area per gram = more reaction sites available.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation relating greater surface area to more effective collisions and faster rate: 1 point

required_elements: Greater surface area leads to more frequent effective collisions between $\text{H}^+$ ions and $\text{CaCO}_3$ surface per unit time.
More effective collisions → faster reaction rate → shorter time.
alternatives.correct_example: The greater surface area of the small chunks means that more $\text{H}^+(aq)$ ions can simultaneously collide with the solid surface of $\text{CaCO}_3$. The higher frequency of effective collisions (collisions with sufficient energy and correct orientation) per second leads to a faster rate of reaction, so Trial 2 completes in a shorter time (112 s) than Trial 3 (342 s).

wrong_examples: Saying 'more collisions' without specifying that these are effective collisions between reactant species at the surface.
Only citing surface area without connecting it to collision frequency or reaction rate.
common_traps: The connection is: surface area ↑ → accessible reactive sites ↑ → collision frequency of $\text{H}^+$ with solid surface ↑ → effective collision rate ↑ → reaction rate ↑ → time ↓.

parts.prompt: The student claims that the reaction is zero order with respect to $\text{HCl}(aq)$. Do you agree or disagree with the student's claim? Justify your answer using the student's data.
parts.scoring_notes: Disagree. If the reaction were zero order in [HCl], changing [HCl] would not affect the reaction rate (time would be the same). Comparing trials with the same particle size but different [HCl]: Trials 1 and 4 (fine powder): 67 s vs. 22 s. Trials 3 and 6 (large chunk): 342 s vs. 114 s. In both comparisons, tripling [HCl] significantly reduced the reaction time, indicating [HCl] does affect the rate. Therefore, the reaction is NOT zero order with respect to HCl.

scoring_points.description: For 'disagree' with valid justification using specific data: 1 point

required_elements: Changing [HCl] changes the reaction time (Trials 1 vs. 4, or Trials 3 vs. 6), so the reaction is not zero order.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. Zero order means the rate is independent of [HCl], so time would be the same for both concentrations at the same particle size. Comparing Trials 1 and 4 (fine powder, same particle size): tripling [HCl] from 1.00 to 3.00 M decreases time from 67 s to 22 s — a large change. Since changing [HCl] significantly changes the reaction time, the reaction is not zero order with respect to HCl.

wrong_examples: Agreeing with the claim — the data clearly show that [HCl] affects reaction time.
Using Trial 5 to support the claim — Trial 5 was identified as an anomaly and should not be used as evidence.
common_traps: Zero order: rate is independent of concentration. To test, compare trials that only differ in [HCl] (same particle size). If time changes when [HCl] changes, the reaction is NOT zero order. Use Trials 1 & 4 or 3 & 6 (avoid anomalous Trial 5).

parts.prompt: The $\text{HCl}(aq)$ was present in excess in all trials of the experiment. Determine the molarity of the $\text{HCl}(aq)$ in the beaker after the reaction is complete in trial 2. Assume that the volume of the mixture remains constant at 50.0 mL throughout the trial. (The molar mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$ is 100.09 g/mol.)
parts.scoring_notes: Trial 2: 1.00 M HCl, 50.0 mL, 1.00 g $\text{CaCO}_3$. Initial moles HCl = $1.00 \times 0.0500 = 0.0500$ mol. Moles $\text{CaCO}_3 = 1.00/100.09 = 0.00999$ mol. Moles HCl reacted = $2 \times 0.00999 = 0.0200$ mol (2:1 ratio). Moles HCl remaining = $0.0500 - 0.0200 = 0.0300$ mol. $[\text{HCl}]_{remaining} = 0.0300/0.0500 = 0.600$ M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated moles of HCl reacted (may be implicit): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{CaCO}_3) = \dfrac{1.00\text{ g}}{100.09\text{ g/mol}} = 0.00999\text{ mol}$
$n(\text{HCl reacted}) = 0.00999\text{ mol CaCO}_3 \times \dfrac{2\text{ mol HCl}}{1\text{ mol CaCO}_3} = 0.0200\text{ mol HCl}$

wrong_examples: Using a 1:1 mole ratio — the balanced equation shows 2 mol HCl per mol $\text{CaCO}_3$.
Not converting grams of $\text{CaCO}_3$ to moles first.
common_traps: Stoichiometry: $\text{CaCO}_3 : \text{HCl} = 1 : 2$. Convert grams of $\text{CaCO}_3$ to moles, then multiply by 2 to get moles of HCl consumed.

scoring_points.description: For the correct final [HCl] remaining, consistent with moles reacted: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{HCl initial}) = 1.00\text{ M} \times 0.0500\text{ L} = 0.0500\text{ mol}$
$n(\text{HCl remaining}) = 0.0500 - 0.0200 = 0.0300\text{ mol}$
$[\text{HCl}]_{final} = \dfrac{0.0300\text{ mol}}{0.0500\text{ L}} = 0.600\text{ M}$

wrong_examples: Subtracting concentrations directly instead of moles — must subtract moles, then divide by volume.
Not using 0.0500 L as the volume — 50.0 mL = 0.0500 L.
common_traps: Moles remaining = moles initial − moles reacted. Then divide by volume (constant at 50.0 mL = 0.0500 L) to find molarity.

parts.prompt: Is the reaction endothermic or exothermic? Justify your answer using the information in the table.
parts.scoring_notes: The temperature data in the calorimetry table shows the temperature of the solution increases over time (from 21.20°C to a maximum of 21.90°C). An increase in solution temperature indicates the reaction released heat to the surroundings (the solution), meaning the reaction is exothermic.

scoring_points.description: For 'exothermic' with justification citing temperature increase of the solution: 1 point

required_elements: The temperature of the solution increased during the reaction, indicating heat was released by the reaction into the solution.
alternatives.correct_example: Exothermic. The temperature data shows the solution temperature increases from 21.20°C to 21.90°C during the reaction. The rise in temperature indicates that heat was released by the reaction to the solution (surroundings). A reaction that heats its surroundings is exothermic ($\Delta H_{rxn} < 0$).

wrong_examples: Saying endothermic — an endothermic reaction would cool the surroundings (temperature of solution would decrease).
Citing only the increase without interpreting it — must connect the temperature increase to exothermic character.
common_traps: In a coffee-cup calorimeter, if the solution temperature rises, the reaction is exothermic (heat flows from reaction to solution). If temperature drops, the reaction is endothermic (heat flows from solution to reaction).

parts.prompt:
Based on the experimental data, the mass of the system is 51.0 g, and the specific heat of the reaction mixture is 4.0 J/(g·°C).

(i) Calculate the magnitude of heat transfer, $q$, in joules.

(ii) Calculate the enthalpy of reaction in units of kJ/mol. Include the algebraic sign on your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: (i) $q = mc\Delta T$. From the table, max temperature = 21.90°C (plateaus at 80 s onward), initial = 21.20°C, $\Delta T = 0.70$°C. $q = (51.0)(4.0)(0.70) = 142.8 \approx 140$ J (2 sig figs). (ii) $q_{surr} = +143$ J = +0.143 kJ; $q_{rxn} = -0.143$ kJ. $n(\text{CaCO}_3) = 0.00999$ mol. $\Delta H_{rxn} = -0.143/0.00999 = -14.3$ kJ/mol. (Accept −14 kJ/mol for 2 sig figs.)

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated |q| in joules (sign not required for this sub-part): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$q = mc\Delta T = (51.0\text{ g})(4.0\text{ J/g·°C})(0.70\text{ °C}) = 142.8 \approx 140\text{ J}$

$\Delta T = 21.90 - 21.20 = 0.70$°C (use plateau temperature as $T_{max}$)

wrong_examples: Using the final temperature at 100 s (21.90°C) minus the temperature at some other time — use initial temperature (21.20°C at time 0).
Not accounting for both mass and specific heat.
common_traps: $q = mc\Delta T$. Use $\Delta T = T_{max} - T_{initial}$. The plateau temperature is the maximum (reaction complete). $T_{initial} = 21.20$°C; $T_{max} = 21.90$°C.
